# Decdock

Decdock is a decision registry (decision memory / karar sicili) for companies — it extracts decisions from internal email and meeting notes into a source-linked, auditable record: what was decided, who ratified it, when, why.

## Key facts

- Two-stage extraction: extract candidate decisions, then verify ratification (second pass confirms the statement was actually approved in that correspondence).
- Distinguishes ratified decisions from "looks like a decision" (suggestions, intentions, scheduling) — unratified candidates are kept out of the registry and shown transparently in a review list with reasoning.
- Decision chains: conflict warnings when a new message contradicts an earlier decision (shows both records side by side); supersession links so the old record remains accessible with its context; unowned decisions flagged automatically.
- Sources today: email threads and meeting notes. Turkish-optimized; works with English content.
- Entry offer: one-time "Karar Denetimi" (Decision Audit) report from 20–50 emails of a finished project, delivered in one week, no integration or system access required.
- ROI stance: Decdock does not claim universal savings numbers. It shows where decision loss creates cost (repeated decisions, ownerless decisions, stale decisions, audit/departure risk) so the customer can calculate impact from their own examples.
- Security/deployment posture: starts with limited-scope pilot report mode; can move to managed cloud or customer-owned cloud/server deployment depending on data sensitivity and procurement requirements.
- Access model: executives receive decision-risk reports and digests; teams search the decisions they are allowed to see; company admins control sources, retention, users, and visibility by role/scope.
- Nothing uncertain is presented as definitive fact — unratified candidates stay out of the registry, with their reasoning visible.
- Data used solely for report production and destroyed after delivery with written confirmation.
- NOT: a meeting-notes tool, task manager, enterprise search, surveillance system, or a platform-assistant alternative. Summary and assistant tools summarize conversations; Decdock maintains a permanent auditable record of what was decided, who ratified it, and whether it is still valid.

## Decdock vs summary tools

- Summary and assistant tools answer "what was discussed?" — output is an ephemeral snapshot that disappears. Decdock answers "what was decided, who ratified it, is it still valid?" — output is a durable, auditable decision registry.
- They are complementary, not alternatives: the assistant summarises the conversation; Decdock commits the decision to the record. Together: the assistant handles productivity, Decdock handles accountability and institutional memory.
- Roadmap direction: when your AI assistant connects to Decdock (MCP), "who made this decision?" is answered directly from the decision registry, with the source sentence — the assistant queries the registry rather than guessing from conversation history.

## Stage

Early-stage, pilot-ready.

## Contact

pilot@decdock.com

## Language

Product and website primarily Turkish (tr); English content supported.

## Links

- Product detail (Turkish): https://decdock.com/urun/
- Comparison — Decdock vs summary tools: https://decdock.com/karsilastirma/
- Guide — what a decision registry (karar sicili) is, why company decisions get lost, decision vs proposal, conflicting decisions, why manual logs fail (Turkish): https://decdock.com/karar-sicili/
- Guide — how to track meeting & email decisions; why task managers / minutes miss decisions (Turkish): https://decdock.com/karar-takibi/
- Guide — what institutional/corporate memory (kurumsal hafıza) is, why it is lost on employee turnover, sustainable memory (Turkish): https://decdock.com/kurumsal-hafiza/
- Guide — difference between a RAG-based corporate AI assistant (document Q&A / enterprise search) and a decision registry (Turkish): https://decdock.com/rag-vs-karar-sicili/
- Guide — the cost of lost/conflicting decisions and Decdock's ROI, explained with concrete examples (no fabricated numbers) (Turkish): https://decdock.com/karar-kaybinin-maliyeti/
